critic_raw: The card fraud detection experiment is carefully designed. The primary metric 'precision at fixed recall (recall=0.80)' requires setting the recall threshold on validation data. If the precision-recall curves of both models have different shapes, the comparison at a single operating point (0.80 recall) may not reflect overall model quality. A model with better AUPRC overall might have lower precision at exactly 0.80 recall. Additionally, the design excludes 'transactions without a resolved label after 90 days' — but in the test window (months 16-18), labels are assigned within 90 days post-transaction. If the test window ends at month 18, transactions from the last 90 days of the test window may not have final labels at evaluation time.
defender_raw: On precision at 0.80 recall: the design includes AUPRC as a secondary metric precisely to assess overall model quality beyond a single operating point. The primary metric (precision at 0.80 recall) reflects the stakeholder's operational constraint — fixed investigator capacity at high recall. Both metrics are reported; the primary metric matches business objectives while AUPRC provides model quality context. On the 90-day label resolution in the test window: the design uses '18-24 months of production data' with the test window being months 16-18. If evaluation occurs when labels are available (90 days after the last test transaction), the test window would have complete labels. The design excludes unresolved labels to avoid label noise — this 90-day wait is factored into the experiment timeline.
adjudication_raw: Two concerns: precision at single operating point vs. AUPRC comparison, and 90-day label resolution timing. Both addressed: AUPRC is secondary metric addressing single operating point limitation; 90-day wait is built into the experiment timeline. Verdict: defense_wins.
